narrative.setting: The Magic School Bus is navigating through Mr. Edgar's bronchial tree when suddenly a bright light floods the airway. A long, flexible tube with a camera at its tip snakes down from above, illuminating everything. Ms. Frizzle gasps with delight: 'A bronchoscope! The doctor is performing bronchoscopy - looking directly into Mr. Edgar's airways. From up in the ICU, they can see everything we're seeing down here. This technology is diagnostic and therapeutic magic.'
narrative.tension: Mr. Edgar has a suspicious lesion in his bronchus. Bronchoscopy will diagnose it and potentially treat it.

scaffolds.narrative: Ms. Frizzle points to the prefix on the bronchoscope. 'Broncho- or bronchi-,' she explains. 'From the Greek bronchos - windpipe, though we now use it for the smaller airways.'
scaffolds.explain: 'BRONCHO-' or 'BRONCHI-' = BRONCHI, the branching airways in the lungs. The bronchi are the tubes that branch from the trachea into the lungs. Think: bronchitis (airway inflammation), bronchodilator (airway widener).

wrongExplains.0: Nose is 'rhino-'. Broncho- refers to the airways deeper in the respiratory tree.
wrongExplains.2: Voice box is 'laryngo-'. Broncho- refers to the airways below the larynx.
wrongExplains.3: Lungs overall are 'pulmo-' or 'pneumo-'. Broncho- specifically refers to the AIRWAYS within the lungs.

scaffolds.narrative: The camera on the bronchoscope swivels, capturing images of the bronchial wall. 'The suffix -scopy,' Ms. Frizzle says. 'One of medicine's most useful suffixes. It means to look, to examine.'
scaffolds.explain: '-SCOPY' = VISUAL EXAMINATION, LOOKING. From Greek 'skopein' = to look at. Bronchoscopy = looking into bronchi. Colonoscopy = looking into colon. Endoscopy = looking inside. Diagnostic technique.

wrongExplains.0: Removal is '-ectomy'. '-scopy' is examination/visualization, not surgery (though procedures can be done during scopy).
wrongExplains.2: Inflammation is '-itis'. '-scopy' is a diagnostic examination technique.
wrongExplains.3: Creating opening is '-ostomy'. '-scopy' is visual examination using a scope.

scaffolds.narrative: The bronchoscope has multiple channels - one for the camera, one for light, one for instruments, one for suction. 'Multifunctional,' Ms. Frizzle marvels. 'Diagnostic AND therapeutic.'
scaffolds.q: Bronchoscopy involves examining the airways using:
options: A flexible or rigid scope with camera and light
scaffolds.explain: BRONCHOSCOPY uses a BRONCHOSCOPE - flexible (most common) or rigid tube with: (1) CAMERA/light for visualization, (2) working channel for instruments (biopsy forceps, brush, lavage), (3) suction. Inserted through mouth/nose or tracheostomy.

wrongExplains.0: X-rays show lungs but don't allow direct visualization. Bronchoscopy uses a CAMERA inside the airways.
wrongExplains.2: Blood tests detect infection/markers. Bronchoscopy is DIRECT VISUALIZATION with a scope.
wrongExplains.3: Stethoscope listens to breath sounds externally. Bronchoscopy LOOKS INSIDE the airways directly.

scaffolds.narrative: The bronchoscope navigates a branching point, choosing the left main bronchus. 'The doctor is steering,' Ms. Frizzle explains. 'Looking for the lesion we spotted on CT scan.'
options: The trachea, bronchi, and smaller airways
scaffolds.explain: Bronchoscopy can visualize: TRACHEA → main bronchi → lobar bronchi → segmental bronchi → subsegmental (depending on scope size). Can see: airway anatomy, tumors, inflammation, foreign bodies, secretions, bleeding sources.

wrongExplains.0: Bronchoscopy goes BEYOND the trachea into the branching bronchi - that's why it's called BRONCHOscopy.
wrongExplains.2: The heart is outside the airways. Bronchoscopy examines the AIRWAY TREE, not the heart.
wrongExplains.3: Stomach is examined by GASTROSCOPY/endoscopy. Bronchoscopy is for AIRWAYS.

scaffolds.narrative: A mass appears on the bronchial wall - irregular, bleeding slightly. Biopsy forceps extend from the bronchoscope, taking a small tissue sample. 'Diagnostic AND therapeutic,' Ms. Frizzle reminds.
scaffolds.q: What procedures can be performed during bronchoscopy?
options: Biopsy, lavage, foreign body removal, stent placement
scaffolds.explain: Bronchoscopy procedures: (1) BIOPSY (tissue samples), (2) LAVAGE (washing for cell/pathogen samples), (3) FOREIGN BODY REMOVAL, (4) stent placement, (5) tumor debulking, (6) hemostasis (stop bleeding). Both diagnostic AND therapeutic.

wrongExplains.0: Bronchoscopy is interventional! Biopsies, lavage, foreign body removal, and treatments can all be performed.
wrongExplains.2: Heart surgery requires opening the chest. Bronchoscopy is minimally invasive, airway-focused.
wrongExplains.3: Brain surgery is neurosurgery. Bronchoscopy examines and treats AIRWAYS.

scaffolds.narrative: Saline is instilled through the bronchoscope, then suctioned back - bronchoalveolar lavage. 'BAL,' Ms. Frizzle says. 'Washing the airways to collect cells and microbes for analysis.'
options: Washing airways with saline to collect cells/pathogens
scaffolds.explain: BAL = instilling SALINE into airways, then suctioning it back to collect: (1) cells (cancer, inflammatory), (2) pathogens (bacteria, fungi, viruses), (3) other material (hemosiderin, proteins). Analyzed in pathology/microbiology labs.

wrongExplains.0: Lung removal is pneumonectomy (major surgery). BAL is minimally invasive washing technique.
wrongExplains.2: Lungs already have air. BAL uses SALINE (liquid) to wash and collect material.
wrongExplains.3: BAL doesn't cut anything. It's a washing/sampling technique, not surgery.

scaffolds.narrative: 'Flexible versus rigid bronchoscopy,' Ms. Frizzle teaches. 'Flexible is more common, better tolerated. Rigid is for massive bleeding, large foreign bodies, or airway interventions.'
scaffolds.q: How does flexible bronchoscopy differ from rigid bronchoscopy?
options: Flexible uses a thin, maneuverable scope; rigid uses a straight metal tube
Flexible requires general anesthesia; rigid doesn't
scaffolds.explain: FLEXIBLE: thin fiberoptic/video scope, maneuverable, reaches subsegmental bronchi, moderate sedation, most common. RIGID: straight metal tube, better for massive bleeding/foreign bodies/stents, general anesthesia, better suctioning capacity.

wrongExplains.0: Very different! Flexible is thin/maneuverable/reaches farther. Rigid is straight/metal/larger working channel.
wrongExplains.2: Opposite! FLEXIBLE uses moderate sedation. RIGID requires general anesthesia (more invasive).
wrongExplains.3: Flexible CAN do biopsies (smaller samples). Rigid can take larger biopsies and handle more bleeding.

scaffolds.narrative: The pathology report returns: lung cancer, adenocarcinoma. 'Early detection,' Ms. Frizzle says gravely. 'Bronchoscopy found it when it was still treatable. That's the power of direct visualization.'
scaffolds.q: Which condition is commonly diagnosed via bronchoscopy?
options: Lung cancer, pneumonia, or interstitial lung disease
scaffolds.explain: Bronchoscopy diagnoses: (1) LUNG CANCER (biopsy), (2) INFECTIONS (BAL for pathogens), (3) INTERSTITIAL LUNG DISEASE (transbronchial biopsy), (4) hemoptysis source, (5) airway obstruction, (6) unexplained infiltrates.

wrongExplains.0: Diabetes is metabolic (blood sugar). Bronchoscopy examines AIRWAYS, not metabolism.
wrongExplains.2: Broken bones need X-rays/CT. Bronchoscopy is for AIRWAY pathology.
wrongExplains.3: Kidney stones are urologic. Bronchoscopy examines RESPIRATORY system.

scaffolds.narrative: A child's bronchoscopy shows a peanut lodged in the right main bronchus. Grasping forceps retrieve it safely. 'Foreign body removal,' Ms. Frizzle says. 'Common in kids. Life-saving procedure.'
scaffolds.q: Bronchoscopy can be used to remove:
options: Foreign bodies (like inhaled food) from airways
scaffolds.explain: Bronchoscopy can remove FOREIGN BODIES from airways: peanuts, coins, teeth, anything inhaled. Common in children. Can also remove mucus plugs, blood clots. Grasping forceps, baskets, or suction used.

wrongExplains.0: Brain tumors need neurosurgery. Bronchoscopy works in AIRWAYS, not brain.
wrongExplains.2: Kidney stones are removed urologically (lithotripsy, ureteroscopy). Bronchoscopy is for AIRWAYS.
wrongExplains.3: Gallstones are removed via ERCP or cholecystectomy. Bronchoscopy is for RESPIRATORY foreign bodies.

scaffolds.narrative: The bronchoscope withdraws. The procedure is complete. Diagnosis made, treatment planned. 'Modern medicine,' Ms. Frizzle reflects. 'Direct visualization changed everything. Final question!'
options: Visual examination of airways using a scope
scaffolds.explain: BRONCHOSCOPY = VISUAL EXAMINATION of the AIRWAYS (trachea, bronchi) using a BRONCHOSCOPE (flexible or rigid scope with camera). Uses: diagnosis (cancer, infection), therapeutic procedures (biopsy, lavage, foreign body removal, stenting). Both diagnostic and interventional.

wrongExplains.0: Removal is bronchectomy. Bronchoscopy is EXAMINATION/visualization, not surgical removal.
wrongExplains.2: Inflammation is bronchitis. Bronchoscopy is a diagnostic/therapeutic PROCEDURE, not an inflammatory condition.
wrongExplains.3: X-ray is radiography (chest X-ray). Bronchoscopy is DIRECT VISUALIZATION with a camera/scope inside the airways.
